title: Python: Display & Variables
description: Learn how to print output and store values in Python
congratsText: Great work! You now know how to display text and use variables in Python.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Use the print() function to display text and numbers on the screen
  • Understand what a variable is and why we use it
  • Create variables and assign values using the = operator
  • Use variables inside print() to display stored values

sections.title: The print() Function

quiz.question: What is the purpose of the print() function in Python?
options: It stores a value in memory for later use
It displays output to the screen or console
It reads input typed by the user at runtime
It imports a module needed for the program

sections.lesson:
The print() Function
print() displays output on the screen — whatever you put inside the parentheses gets shown.
print("Hello, World!") # Output: Hello, World!

sections.title: Printing Text (Strings)

quiz.question: What will this code display on the screen?
options: "Hello, World!" — including the quotation marks
Hello, World! — the text without quotes
Hello — only the first word before the comma
Nothing — print() needs parentheses to work

sections.lesson:
Printing Text (Strings)
Text in Python is called a string — wrap it in single or double quotes to print it.
print("Hello!") print('Python is fun') # Quotes are not shown in the output

sections.title: Printing Numbers & Expressions

quiz.question: What does Python display when this code runs?
options: 10 + 5 — the expression as typed in the code
15 — Python evaluates the expression first
105 — the two numbers joined as a string
Error — print() cannot handle math inside it

sections.lesson:
Printing Numbers & Expressions
Numbers don't need quotes — Python also evaluates math expressions inside print() before displaying the result.
print(42) print(10 + 5) # Output: 42 then 15

sections.title: Printing Multiple Values

quiz.question: What does this code print?
options: Score:95 — the values joined with no space
Score: 95 — a space is added between values
Score — only the string part is displayed
Error — print() cannot mix text and numbers

sections.lesson:
Printing Multiple Values
Separate multiple values with commas inside print() — Python automatically adds a space between them.
print("Score:", 95) # Output: Score: 95

sections.title: What is a Variable?

quiz.question: What is a variable in Python?
options: A fixed value that cannot change after creation
A named container that stores a value in memory
A built-in function used to display output
A keyword that imports data from a file

sections.lesson:
What is a Variable?
A variable is a named container that stores a value in memory so you can reuse it later.
score = 85 print(score) # Output: 85

sections.title: Creating Variables

quiz.question: What value is stored in the variable after this line runs?
options: The text 'score' — the variable name is stored
85 — the integer value assigned to score
Nothing — = just declares an empty variable
True — Python confirms the assignment worked

sections.lesson:
Creating Variables
Use = to create a variable and assign it a value — Python automatically detects the data type.
name = "Ana" age = 20 grade = 95.5 print(name, age, grade) # Output: Ana 20 95.5

sections.title: Variable Naming Rules

quiz.question: Which of these is a valid Python variable name?
options: 2score — starts with a number, not a letter
student_name — uses letters and underscore
my score — contains a space between words
class — this is a reserved Python keyword

sections.lesson:
Variable Naming Rules
Variable names must start with a letter or underscore, contain no spaces, and cannot be a Python keyword.
# Valid student_name = "Ana" # Invalid 2score = 85 # starts with a number my score = 20 # contains a space

sections.title: Printing Variables

quiz.question: What does this code display?
options: Hello, name — the variable name text is displayed
Hello, Ana — the value stored in name is displayed
Hello — only the very first argument is displayed
Error — variables cannot be used inside print() here

sections.lesson:
Printing Variables
Use the variable name inside print() without quotes to display its value — or use an f-string for clean formatting.
name = "Ana" print("Hello,", name) # Hello, Ana print(f"Hi, {name}!") # Hi, Ana!

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: Which line correctly stores the text 'Python' in a variable and prints it?
options: language == 'Python' then print(language)
language = 'Python' then print(language)
print(language) then language = 'Python'
var language = 'Python' then print(language)

sections.lesson:
Recap
Use print() to display text, numbers, and variables — and use variables with = to store and reuse values.
language = "Python" print("I am learning", language) # Output: I am learning Python
